'California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
September 74, 1990
Bob connelly
Chief Adninistrative officer
Assembly Rules committee
state capitoL -- Room 3016
Sacramento, CA 94249-OoOl
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-9 0-52 4
Dear !lr. Connelly:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning the
application of the mass mailing provisions of the political 
Reform
act (tne "Act'r).1 Specifically, we are providing this advice with
respect to descriptions of typical meetings held by Assembly
nembers submitted to us by Mr. Bill Cavala. Because this advice
j.s rendered to assist you in advising members of the Assembly, s/e
are treating this request as one for inforrnaf assistance pursuant
to Regulation 18239(c) (r) (copy enclosed).2
PRELII,{INARY DTSCUSSION
According to information that you have given us, it is conrnon
for legisJ,ators to hold meetings centered around a single issue or
set of issues. The l-egislator arranges the tine and place,
invites participants, arranetes the rneeting schedule, introduces
speakers, determines the services or i,nformation to be provided,
obtains insurance and is present to meet with constituents and
discuss legislation.
1 cor"rrrr"nt Code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornrnission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seq. Al1 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 rnforrual assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnmunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(covernment code Section 83114:, 2 CaL. code of Regs.
Section 1,8329 (c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O? o Sacramento CA 958O4'O8O7 . (916) 322'5660


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-524
Page 2
Mr. Bill Cavala of the Assenbly staff has furnished descrip-
tions of typical rneetings held by members of the Assembly. You
wish to know if notices announcing these meetinqs may be sent to a
Iegislator's constituents pursuant to an exception to the
prohibition on mass rnailings. (section 89oo1; Regulation 189o1,
copy enclosed. ) SpecificallY, You wish to know if the exception
pernitting announcements of constituent neetings (Regnrlation
18e01(b) (e) (A) (1.)) is applicable.
Section 89001 states that r'no newsletter or other mass mail-
ing sha11 be sent at pubtic expense.rr A mass mailing is defined
as tt o hundred or more substantially similar pieces of maiI.
(Section 82041.5.) Applied literatly, 
this section would prohibit
all mass mailing invoJ.ving public funds, irrespective of content
or purpose, including such items as tax notices, college
schedules, sample ballots and other mass mailj.ngs sent by govern-
ment agencies. Regulation l-8901 clarifies that application of the
prohibition is linited to j,tens sent which include references to
elected officers affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the nailing.3
Subdivision (b) of Regulation 18901 sets forth limited excep-
tions to the general prohibition of section 89001. we have
consistently provided conservative advice in applying these excep-
tions on the premise that exceptions should be narrowly construed.
(Estate of Baneriee (1978) 2L ca1.3d 527,54o.)
one of the exceptions included in Regulation 18901 pertains
to the sending of constituent neeting notices. 
(Regulation
18901(b) (9) (A) (1.).) 
Under this exception, mass maiLinq of the
following is not prohibited by Section 890013
An announcenent sent to an elected officer's
constituents concerning a public meeting which is
directty related to the elected officer's 
incurnbent
governmental duties, which is to be held by the
elected officer, and which the elected officer
intends to attend.
Any such announcenent nay not include the elected officer's
photograph or signature and rnay include only a single mention of
the elected officer's 
name, except as other$rise permitted by the
reguLation. (Regulation 18901(b) (9) (B) . )
The meeting notice exception requires that the foJ,lowing
elenents be present:
1. The announcenent must be sent to the elected officerrs
constituents;
3 Th" Commission,s authority to
the absurd results of its literal
v. Fair Pol,itical Practices Con.
interpret section 8900■ to avoid
app■icatiOn was upheld in Watson
(■990)2■7 Cal.App.3d 1059.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-524
Page 3
2. It must concern a public meetinq directlv related to the
elected officer's 
incumbent qovernmental duties;
3. The meeting must be held bv the elected officer;
4. The elected officer nust intend to attend the neeting.
',gg.ng!i.tllClf!-q" : we have given telephone advice that
rr consti.tuentsrr means the voters in the elected officer's district.
Basically, we would include anyone in the district, 
whether or not
the person is actually registered to vote. For example, we have
given telephone advice that an elected officer could send a nail--
ing using district 
property ro11s, even though this might result
in nailings to absentee oe/ners living outsj,de the district.
ttPublic rneetinq. . .directlv related to. . . incunbent
g.9yC.511Ml&e].-gg!.ieg": There should be some type of assembly
providing an encounter between the elected officer and his or her
constituents. The announced meeting should be open to the Pub1ic
and must relate to the governmentat duties of the elected officer
holding the neeting. For exanple, the neeting may focus on
constituent concerns and the need for legislation or other action
to remedy theni the elected officer may discuss pending legisla-
tion and how it will affect the district; 
the el-ected officer nay
explain the effect of legislation which has been adopted.
ttHeld bv the eLected officer'r: 
A neeting is held by an
elected officer when the elected officer exercises direction and
control over the agenda, is responsible for rnaking all physical
and financiaL arrangenents for the rneeting, and conducts the
neeting. A neeting is not hetd by an elected officer if the
elected officer merely participates in an event put on by someone
el,se.
',IgEetg_39_3!!elfq,': The elected of f icer shouLd intend to be
present for al.I or substantially all of the neeting.
OUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS
with the foregoing in mind, Ire now review the neeting
descriptions presented. The advice provided is prospective only
and is not intended as a conment with respect to any past neetings
which may have been noticed by an elected officer.l
4 w" h.r" also been provided with exanples of meeting notices
that have apparently been sent. we note that many of these
notices were sent subsequent to the adoption of Regulation 18901.
We decline to comment on any specific notice since this would
violate our policy of not advising regarding past conduct.
(Regulation 18329 (b) (8) (A) and (c) (4) (A) . )


Our Fi■e No. I-90-524
Page 4
For purposes of our review, we assume that the member ar-
ranges the tine and place of the meeti.ng, invites the
participants, arranges the neeting schedule, introduces the speak-
ers, determines the services and information to be provided,
obtains insurance and is present to meet uith constituents and
discuss legislation. 
we further assume that the member is present
for all or substantially all of the roeeting; the neeting is open
to the public; and the notice is only sent to the elected
officerTs rrconst j.tuentsrr as defined above. our analysis tri1l
focus upon rrhether the event is held by the elected officer and is
directly related to the elected officer's 
incurnbent governmental
duties.
1. census as/areness lrorkshops: designed to bring cornmunity
groups that prepare unemployed people together with census bureau
for hiring purposes.
Based upon the assumptj.ons set forth above, so long as the
eLected officer conducts the workshop, this would apPear to be a
meeting held by the elected offj'cer' 
Ho$rever, it isn't clear frorn
the information provided what connection there is between the
elected offj.cer's 
rr incumbent governmental dutiestr and the
workshops. Without more information, vre cannot advj'se $rhether
this neeting woul-d fal-I within the exception.
2. I'{easles inoculation centers: designed to provide free
measles cfinics, vaccine to poor population unable to attend clin-
ics which are not open on weekends and are distant from epidenic
population. Member sponsored legislation rnandating second measles
shot and would attend al,1 cl-inics.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so fong as the
elected officer conducts the clinics, they would appear to be held
by the elected officer. 
Holrever, in order to constitute a |tmeet-
ing," there rnust be either sorne assembly or other opportunity for
inaividuals or groups to rrneetrr wj.th the elected officer. 
Since
the elected officer sponsored the legislation mandating the
vaccinations, the officer's presence at meetings inplenenting the
program would appear to be directly related to his or her
legislative program.
3. I{alathion meetinqs: held by members supporting legisla-
tion that would restrict adroinistration's ability to spray without
environmental reports. 
lilenber provides infornational naterial
about malathion, status report on legislation.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, if the member
conducts the neeting, it would be held by the el-ected officer.
Providing infornation and a status report about a legislative
progran sponsored by the Dember would appear to be directly
related to the menber's incunbent governrnental duties.
4. Smal1 business conferences: members sponsor with agen-
cies and groups in the private sector to provide information about


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-524
Page 5
state progEams of assistance to smal1 business, tax information,
new legislation.
we note that the event is cosponsored. However, the fact
that the event is cosponsored does not, in and of itself, 
preclude
a deternination that the conference is held by the elected
officer. 
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, it is the
elected officer, and not the cosponsor, who has direction and
control over the agenda and is responsible for naking all physical
and financial arrangements. so long as the elected officer also
conducts the conference, it is held by the elected officer.
The conference would clearly be directly related to the
member,s incumbent governmental duties if the conference focuses
on input for ne!, legislation to be sponsored by the nember,.
legislatj.on currently being carried by the member, legislation
cairied by the member which was recently passed, or legislation
currently before a committee or subcommittee of which the elected
officer is a mernber. However, it is unclear from the inforuration
presented how the meeting would directly relate to the member's
-incumbent governmental duties to the extent that it merely gives
private groups an opportunity to conduct semj.nars of interest to
sma1l business. we have previously advised that such conferences
do not appear to fa11 within the exception. (Leslie Advice Let-
ter, No. A-89-521, coPY enclosed.)
5. Government dav: mernber sponsors a meeting inviting
dozens of government agencies to present their wares to the
public. 
Menber also attends, openingT a district 
office booth to
accept new ideas for state legislation.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so long as the
nember conducts the neeting, i.t is hel,d by the member. rroviding
his or her constituents access to governmental agencies and
obtaining ideas for new legislation tould appear to be directly
related to incurnbent governmental duties of the member.
6. Senior fair: 
member sponsors a ureeting inviting agencies
that specialize in services to senior citizens to present their
rrares to senj,ors. Bills of interest to the senior population are
provided and ideas for nes, bi1ls solicited.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so long as the
member conducts the rneeting, it is held by the member. Assisting
his or her constituents to obtain access to governmental agencies,
obtaining ideas for ne!, bills to be carried by the nenber and
explaining new or pending legislation to constituents would appear


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-524
Page 6
to be directly related to the member's incumlrent governmental du-
ties . 5
7. Child care: member sponsors a neeting inviting agencies
that specialize in chj.ld care services to present their.wares to
young ianilies. 
Infonnation on child care legislation is
provided.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so long as the
member conducts the ureeting, it is held by the nember. Providing
his or her constituents access to governnental agencies, obtaining
ideas for new bills to be carried by the member and explainingt
new or pending legistation to constituents would appear to be
ctirectly relaaed io the offi,cer's incumbent governmental- duties.
8. Firearms safetv meetino: member cal1s a rneeting of
constituents interested in the firearms legislative package.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so l-ong as the
member conduLts the meeting, it is held by the menber. Meeting
with interested constituents to discuss pending legislatj-on would
appear to be directly related to the menber's incunbent
governmental duties.
g. Mobile home residents: residents are invited to legisla-
tive rneetings featuring the services of specific interest to them;
new legislaiion is provided; j'deas for further legislation are
sought.
Based upon the assumptions set forth above, so long as the
elected offiler 
conducts the neeting, it is held by the elected
officer. 
Meeting with constituents to discuss new legislation and
ideas for future legislation would appear to be directly related
to the nember's incumbent governmental duties.
Finally, the information from Mr. cavala indicates that nany
of the above ureetings are sinilar--on a smaller, localized scale--
to legislative hearings held in Sacramento. For exampfe, an
interirn hearing on conuron interest subdivisions held by the As-
' 
we have previously advised that a notice of a senior fair was
not within Lhe exception because the fair was an ttinf orroational
event. rr (Eaves Advice Letter, No. I-89-189, copy encLosed.) We
have subsequently determined that the notice exception is more
appropriately analyzed as set forth herein. However, events must
be analyzed on a case-by-case basis to deterrnine whether they are
the tlT)e of event that nay be announced using the exception for
constituent neetings. our assessnent of the senior fair
described, subject to the assumptions set forth, is not intended
to be deterrninative with respect to other senior fairs which nay
be structured differently and in which the elected officer's
involvement nay vary.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-524
Page 7
senbly Housing Cornmittee may be duplicated in various districts
that have a high density of residents living in such developments.
The similarity to legislative hearings noted by Mr. cavala
coincides with our general interpretation of the constituent meet-
ing exemption. The elected officer is present and in charge of
the roeeting. The elected officer exercises direction and control
over the agenda and is responsible for naking the physical and
financial arranqenents for the neeting. The neeting relates to
the member's incumbent governmental duties.
I trust that the foregoing has adequately responded to your
request for advj.ce as to the applicability of the constituent
neeting exemption to announcenents of the types of events set
forth. 
If you have any questions, please contact ne at (916) 322-
5901.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
―
〃〆
By: Margaret w. Ellison
counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: Whether notices announcing various types of constituent meetings held by Assembly members may be sent to constituents pursuant to the exception to the prohibition on mass mailings under Political Reform Act Section 89001 and Regulation 18901(b)(9)(A)(1).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides prospective advice on six types of meetings (census workshops, measles inoculation centers, malathion meetings, small business conferences, government day, and senior fair), concluding that notices for most can qualify for the constituent meeting exception if the member exercises direction and control over the meeting and it directly relates to the member's incumbent governmental duties, though some require additional information or analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is informal advice (not formal opinion), dated September 1990. No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers, but content clearly identifies the legal question and provides detailed analysis of six meeting scenarios. Analysis sections are formatted as numbered items rather than formal conclusion sections. Document appears truncated in provided text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether notices announcing various types of constituent meetings held by Assembly members may be sent to constituents pursuant to the exception to the prohibition on mass mailings under Political Reform Act Section 89001 and Regulation 18901(b)(9)(A)(1).

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provides prospective advice on six types of meetings (census workshops, measles inoculation centers, malathion meetings, small business conferences, government day, and senior fair), concluding that notices for most can qualify for the constituent meeting exception if the member exercises direction and control over the meeting and it directly relates to the member's incumbent governmental duties, though some require additional information or analysis.

embedding.summary: This FPPC informal advice letter addresses whether Assembly members may send mass mailing notices for various constituent meetings without violating the Political Reform Act's prohibition on mass mailings at public expense, analyzing six specific meeting types against the regulatory exception for constituent meeting announcements.
